concerns.text: SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤ 0.01, while FR-026 and SC-026 specify ≤ 0.005. This creates a conflicting requirement and makes SC-003 untestable.

FR-002 references 'data-model.md' and 'extracted_summary.schema.yaml' which are external files not included in the spec. These references should either be self-contained or clearly indicate how to access these external dependencies.
FR-030 cites 'Kohavi et al. (2020)' but the reference lacks full bibliographic details. Constitution Principle II requires verified citations with title-token-overlap ≥ 0.7.
SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤ 0.01, but FR-026 (which SC-026 anchors to) specifies ≤ 0.005. This creates an untestable contradiction – which threshold governs verification?
SC-026 references FR-026, but does not resolve the threshold conflict between SC-003 and FR-026. The success criteria should align with the functional requirements.
SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤ 0.01, while SC-026 and FR-026 specify ≤ 0.005. This creates a conflicting requirement.

FR-002 references 'data-model.md' and 'extracted_summary.schema.yaml' which are external files not included in the spec. Cross-references should be self-contained or clearly indicate external dependencies.

FR-030 cites 'Kohavi et al. (2020)' but the reference section only contains a brief note without full bibliographic details. Constitution Principle II requires verified citations with title-token-overlap ≥ 0.7.
SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤ 0.01, but FR-026 specifies ≤ 0.005. This creates an untestable contradiction — which threshold governs verification?
SC-026 references FR-026 but does not resolve the threshold conflict with SC-003. The differing thresholds create ambiguity and a potential failure point.

SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤ 0.01, but FR-026 and SC-026 specify ≤ 0.005 for the same validation. This creates an untestable contradiction — which threshold governs verification? Must be resolved to a single value.

SC-026 references FR-026 but does not resolve the threshold conflict with SC-003 (0.005 vs 0.01). Both SCs must agree on the same Monte Carlo validation threshold for the requirement to be verifiable.

FR-002 references 'data-model.md' and 'extracted_summary.schema.yaml' which are external files not included in the spec. Cross-references must either be self-contained or clearly indicate external dependencies that will be provided.

FR-030 cites 'Kohavi et al. (2020)' but lacks full bibliographic details (title, venue, DOI). Constitution Principle II requires verified citations with title-token-overlap ≥ 0.7. Add complete reference or remove citation.

FR-026 mandates Monte Carlo validation with 10,000 replicates and threshold ≤ 0.005, but SC-003 uses threshold ≤ 0.01. The success criterion must match the functional requirement for the requirement to be verifiable.

Unresolved threshold conflict: SC-003 specifies Monte Carlo validation threshold ≤0.01, SC-026 specifies ≤0.005, FR-026 states ≤0.005. These conflicting thresholds create ambiguity about which success criterion governs verification. This was flagged in the kickback and remains unresolved.

FR-002 references external files 'data-model.md' and 'extracted_summary.schema.yaml' not included in the spec. Cross-references should either be self-contained or clearly indicate external dependencies. This was flagged in the kickback and remains unresolved.

FR-030 cites 'Kohavi et al. (2020)' but lacks full bibliographic details. Constitution Principle II requires verified citations with title-token-overlap ≥0.7. This was flagged in the kickback and remains unresolved.

SC-003 and SC-026 both reference Monte Carlo validation but with different thresholds (0.01 vs 0.005). SC-026 references FR-026 but does not resolve the threshold conflict. This creates an untestable contradiction.

FR-026 mandates Monte Carlo validation with 10,000 replicates, but SC-003 uses a different threshold (0.01 vs 0.005). The spec does not clarify which threshold governs verification, leaving the success criterion ambiguous.

FR-030/FR-031/SC-030 create circular validation: synthetic dataset generated by the system's own validation framework (using 'independent library' but same system) validates the inconsistency detector. Precision/recall targets (≥90%/≥80%) are self-fulfilling—tuning synthetic data generation can meet them without testing real-world performance. This doesn't validate against actual public A/B test summaries.

FR-005a asserts baseline proportion of 0.05 'justified by prior meta-analysis of reporting errors in A/B testing' but cites no verified source. Scope Justification mentions 'John et al., 2022' without full bibliographic details. Constitution Principle II requires verified citations with title-token-overlap ≥0.7. The baseline is empirically ungrounded.

FR-032 performs Fisher's exact tests across multiple subgroups (by domain, by year) without multiple testing correction. Assumptions state 'no additional corrections are applied'—this inflates Type I error rate. Standard practice requires Bonferroni or FDR correction when conducting multiple hypothesis tests.

FR-012 uses 'average of the two variant rates' to reconstruct effect size when baseline is missing. This is a heuristic approximation, not statistically rigorous. If baseline is missing, effect size reconstruction is fundamentally compromised—this limitation should be acknowledged rather than treated as equivalent to complete data.

Core methodology tests internal consistency (reported vs. reconstructed from same reported data) but cannot detect cases where all numbers are wrong but internally consistent. This fundamental limitation is not acknowledged. The research question asks about 'statistical consistency' but the methodology only validates internal arithmetic, not external validity against ground truth.

FR-030/FR-031/SC-030 still create circular validation: synthetic dataset generated by the system's own validation framework validates the inconsistency detector. Adding a limitation note acknowledges the problem but does not resolve the scientific soundness issue. Precision/recall targets on synthetic data cannot validate real-world performance. Need either: (1) truly independent ground-truth dataset, (2) remove synthetic precision/recall as validation of real-world performance, or (3) add real-world validation with manually annotated ground truth.

responses.response: Unified Monte Carlo validation threshold to ≤0.005 across FR-026, SC-003, and SC-026 to eliminate contradiction.

new_concerns.text: FR-030/FR-031/SC-030 still create circular validation: synthetic dataset generated by the system's own validation framework validates the inconsistency detector. Adding a limitation note acknowledges the problem but does not resolve the scientific soundness issue. Precision/recall targets on synthetic data cannot validate real-world performance. Need either: (1) truly independent ground-truth dataset, (2) remove synthetic precision/recall as validation of real-world performance, or (3) add real-world validation with manually annotated ground truth.

responses.response: Added FR-031b and SC-031b to require real-world validation with manually annotated ground truth for inconsistency detection. Clarified that synthetic validation (FR-030/031/SC-030) tests implementation correctness only, while FR-031b/SC-031b validates real-world detection performance.
responses.what_changed: Added FR-031b (real-world validation with ≥100 manually annotated summaries, precision≥85%, recall≥75%), SC-031b (corresponding success criterion), updated FR-031 limitation note, updated SC-030 to clarify synthetic-only scope
